back_translation: Jacob lived in the land where his father had dwelt, in the land of Canaan.

These are the generations of Jacob. Joseph, being seventeen years old, was tending the flock with his brothers, and he was a youth with the sons of Bilhah and the sons of Zilpah, his father's wives. Joseph brought a bad report of them to their father.
Israel loved Joseph more than all his sons, because he was the son of his old age, and he made him a robe of many colors.
When his brothers saw that their father loved him more than all his brothers, they hated him and could not speak to him peaceably.
Joseph dreamed a dream and told it to his brothers, and they hated him even more.
He said to them, "Listen to this dream that I have dreamed:
Behold, we were binding sheaves in the middle of the field, and behold, my sheaf rose up and stood upright, and behold, your sheaves gathered around and bowed down to my sheaf."
His brothers said to him, "Will you indeed reign over us? Will you indeed rule over us?" So they hated him even more for his dreams and his words.
Joseph dreamed yet another dream and told it to his brothers, and said, "Behold, I have dreamed another dream, and behold, the sun and the moon and eleven stars were bowing down to me."

He told it to his father and his brothers, and his father rebuked him and said to him, "What is this dream that you have dreamed? Will I and your mother and your brothers indeed come to bow down before you to the ground?"
His brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept the matter in mind.
His brothers went to pasture their father's flock at Shechem.
Israel said to Joseph, "Are not your brothers pasturing the flock at Shechem? Come, let me send you to them." He said to him, "Here I am."
He said to him, "Go please, and see how your brothers are doing and how the flock is, and bring me word." So he sent him from the Valley of Hebron, and he came to Shechem.
A man found him wandering in the field, and the man asked him, "What are you seeking?"
He said, "I am seeking my brothers. Please tell me where they are pasturing."
The man said, "They have moved on from here, for I heard them say, 'Let us go to Dothan.'" So Joseph went after his brothers and found them at Dothan.
They saw him from afar, and before he came near to them, they conspired against him to kill him.
They said to one another, "Behold, this dreamer is coming."
Now then, come, let us kill him and throw him into one of the pits, and we will say that a wild beast has devoured him, and we shall see what becomes of his dreams."
When Reuben heard, he rescued him from their hand and said, "Let us not strike him to death."
Reuben said to them, "Do not shed blood. Throw him into this pit which is in the wilderness, but do not lay a hand on him" — that he might rescue him from their hand and bring him back to his father.
When Joseph came to his brothers, they stripped Joseph of his robe — the robe of many colors that he wore.
They took him and threw him into the pit. The pit was empty; there was no water in it.

They sat down to eat bread, and they lifted up their eyes and saw, and behold, a caravan of Ishmaelites was coming from Gilead, with their camels bearing spices and balm and myrrh, going down to Egypt.
Judah said to his brothers, "What profit is it if we kill our brother and conceal his blood?
Come, let us sell him to the Ishmaelites, and let our hand not be upon him, for he is our brother, our flesh." His brothers listened.
Midianite traders passed by, and they pulled and lifted up Joseph from the pit, and they sold Joseph to the Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver, and they brought Joseph to Egypt.
Reuben returned to the pit, and behold, Joseph was not in the pit, and he tore his clothes.
He returned to his brothers and said, "The boy is gone! And I — where shall I go?"
They took Joseph's robe, slaughtered a male goat, and dipped the robe in the blood.
They sent the robe of many colors and brought it to their father and said, "This we found. Please identify it — is it your son's robe or not?"
He recognized it and said, "It is my son's robe. A wild beast has devoured him. Joseph has surely been torn to pieces."
Jacob tore his garments, put sackcloth on his loins, and mourned for his son many days.
All his sons and all his daughters rose to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted, and said, "For I shall go down to my son mourning to Sheol." So his father wept for him.
The Medanites sold him in Egypt to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, captain of the guard.
